Describe when to use VPC Peering and its limitations

activities.description: Using AWS CLI commands, create a VPC with a public and private subnet in two AZs. Add an IGW, NAT Gateway, and correct route tables. Verify by launching an instance in the private subnet and confirming it can reach the internet via NAT GW but is not directly reachable.

Design security group rules for a 3-tier app: (1) ALB SG: accept 443 from 0.0.0.0/0. (2) App SG: accept 8080 from ALB SG only. (3) DB SG: accept 5432 from App SG only. Write the AWS CLI commands for each.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a public subnet and a private subnet in AWS?:::A public subnet has a route in its route table directing 0.0.0.0/0 traffic to an Internet Gateway (IGW). Instances in a public subnet with a public IP can send and receive traffic from the internet directly. A private subnet has no route to an IGW — its 0.0.0.0/0 route (if any) points to a NAT Gateway, allowing outbound-only internet access. Instances in private subnets are not directly reachable from the internet.

Why must you add inbound AND outbound rules in a NACL when allowing web traffic, but only an inbound rule in a Security Group?:::Security Groups are stateful — they track connection state. If an inbound connection is allowed, the return traffic is automatically permitted regardless of outbound rules. NACLs are stateless — they evaluate each packet independently with no connection tracking. For a client connecting to port 80, you must allow: inbound TCP port 80 AND outbound TCP ephemeral ports 1024-65535 (the client's response port). Without the outbound ephemeral port rule, the response is dropped.

What are VPC Endpoints and why should you use them for S3 access from a private subnet?:::Without a VPC Endpoint, traffic from a private subnet to S3 goes out through the NAT Gateway to the public internet and back. This incurs NAT Gateway data processing charges and exposes S3 traffic to the public internet. A Gateway VPC Endpoint for S3 creates a private route in your route table that keeps S3 traffic within the AWS network — no NAT Gateway, no internet exposure, and no cost for the endpoint itself. Bucket policies can also require access only from your VPC Endpoint, preventing accidental public access.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a secure multi-AZ VPC from scratch with proper subnet segmentation, security group chaining, NAT Gateway, and VPC endpoints. Can explain Security Group vs NACL in an interview.

sessions.title: Messaging: SQS, SNS, and EventBridge
sessions.paretoJustification: Decoupled messaging is core to every distributed AWS architecture. SQS, SNS, and EventBridge appear in almost every system-design interview involving AWS, and understanding their trade-offs is essential for senior-level discussions.
objectives: Distinguish SQS Standard queues from FIFO queues and know when to use each
Implement the fanout pattern using SNS + SQS subscriptions
Configure dead-letter queues (DLQ) and understand redrive policies
Compare Kinesis Data Streams with SQS for streaming vs. queuing workloads
Build event-driven rules with EventBridge to route events between AWS services

activities.description: Create a Standard SQS queue and a FIFO queue via CLI, send messages, and observe ordering guarantees
Build a fanout architecture: one SNS topic subscribed to by two SQS queues (email and order-processing)
Attach a DLQ to a queue, deliberately poison messages, and inspect failed messages in the DLQ
Create an EventBridge rule that matches S3 PutObject events and routes them to a Lambda target

sessions.content:
## SQS: Simple Queue Service

SQS is a fully managed message queue that decouples producers and consumers. Messages persist up to 14 days. Visibility timeout (default 30 s) hides a message from other consumers while one consumer is processing it — if processing fails, the message reappears.

```bash
# Create a Standard queue
aws sqs create-queue --queue-name orders

# Create a FIFO queue (name MUST end in .fifo)
aws sqs create-queue \
  --queue-name orders.fifo \
  --attributes FifoQueue=true,ContentBasedDeduplication=true

# Send a message
aws sqs send-message \
  --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/orders \
  --message-body '{"orderId": "ORD-001", "amount": 49.99}'

# Receive messages (long-polling, waits up to 20 s)
aws sqs receive-message \
  --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/orders \
  --wait-time-seconds 20 \
  --max-number-of-messages 10

# Delete a processed message
aws sqs delete-message \
  --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/orders \
  --receipt-handle "<ReceiptHandle from receive response>"
```

### Standard vs. FIFO Queues

| Feature | Standard | FIFO |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | Unlimited (nearly) | 300 msg/s (3000 with batching) |
| Ordering | Best-effort | Strict FIFO within a group |
| Delivery | At-least-once | Exactly-once |
| Use case | High-throughput, order-tolerant | Financial transactions, inventory |

### Dead-Letter Queues (DLQ)

A DLQ captures messages that fail processing after N attempts (maxReceiveCount). Both Standard and FIFO queues support DLQs.

```bash
# Create DLQ first
aws sqs create-queue --queue-name orders-dlq

# Get DLQ ARN
DLQ_ARN=$(aws sqs get-queue-attributes \
  --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/orders-dlq \
  --attribute-names QueueArn \
  --query Attributes.QueueArn --output text)

# Attach DLQ with redrive policy (max 3 retries)
aws sqs set-queue-attributes \
  --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/orders \
  --attributes "{\"RedrivePolicy\":\"{\\\"deadLetterTargetArn\\\":\\\"$DLQ_ARN\\\",\\\"maxReceiveCount\\\":\\\"3\\\"}\"}" 
```

## SNS: Simple Notification Service — Fanout Pattern

SNS is a pub/sub service. Publishers send to a topic; subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS) all receive a copy. This fanout pattern decouples the publisher from N consumers.

```bash
# Create SNS topic
aws sns create-topic --name order-events

# Subscribe an SQS queue to the topic
aws sns subscribe \
  --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789:order-events \
  --protocol sqs \
  --notification-endpoint arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789:orders

# Publish an event
aws sns publish \
  --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789:order-events \
  --message '{"orderId": "ORD-001"}' \
  --subject "NewOrder"
```

**Fanout pattern:** `OrderService → SNS topic → [SQS inventory queue, SQS email queue, SQS analytics queue]`. Each queue is processed by a separate service independently.

## EventBridge

EventBridge is a serverless event bus. It routes events from AWS services, SaaS apps, or custom applications using JSON-pattern rules.

```bash
# Create a custom event bus
aws events create-event-bus --name my-app-bus

# Create a rule: match S3 PutObject on a specific bucket
aws events put-rule \
  --name s3-upload-rule \
  --event-bus-name my-app-bus \
  --event-pattern '{
    "source": ["aws.s3"],
    "detail-type": ["Object Created"],
    "detail": { "bucket": { "name": ["my-uploads-bucket"] } }
  }' \
  --state ENABLED

# Add Lambda as target
aws events put-targets \
  --rule s3-upload-rule \
  --event-bus-name my-app-bus \
  --targets '[{"Id": "1", "Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:function:ProcessUpload"}]'
```

## Kinesis vs SQS

| Aspect | SQS | Kinesis Data Streams |
|---|---|---|
| Model | Queue (each message consumed once) | Log (replay from any offset) |
| Consumers | One consumer per message | Multiple consumers, each reads full stream |
| Retention | Up to 14 days | 1–365 days |
| Ordering | FIFO only with FIFO queue | Per-shard ordering |
| Use case | Task queues, work distribution | Real-time analytics, event sourcing |

**Rule of thumb:** Use SQS when you want messages consumed once by one worker. Use Kinesis when multiple services need to independently read the same stream (e.g., analytics + ML + archival simultaneously).

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Main {
    // Simple message queue with producer-consumer pattern
    static final BlockingQueue<String> queue = new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(10);

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Producer
        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
                try {
                    queue.put("msg-" + i);
                    System.out.println("Produced: msg-" + i);
                } catch (InterruptedException e) { break; }
            }
        });

        // Consumer
        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
                try {
                    String msg = queue.take();
                    System.out.println("Consumed: " + msg);
                } catch (InterruptedException e) { break; }
            }
        });

        producer.start(); consumer.start();
        producer.join(); consumer.join();
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

```python
import queue
import threading

q = queue.Queue(maxsize=10)

def producer():
    for i in range(5):
        q.put(f'msg-{i}')
        print(f'Produced: msg-{i}')

def consumer():
    for _ in range(5):
        msg = q.get()
        print(f'Consumed: {msg}')
        q.task_done()

t1 = threading.Thread(target=producer)
t2 = threading.Thread(target=consumer)
t1.start(); t2.start()
t1.join(); t2.join()
```
reviewQuestions: What is the difference between SQS Standard and FIFO queues?:::Standard queues offer unlimited throughput and at-least-once, best-effort ordering. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once delivery and strict FIFO ordering within a message group, but are capped at 300 msg/s (3,000 with batching). Use FIFO for financial transactions or any case where duplicate or out-of-order messages cause data corruption.

Explain the SQS visibility timeout and what happens if a consumer crashes.:::When a consumer receives a message, SQS hides it for the visibility timeout duration (default 30 s). If the consumer deletes the message before the timeout, processing is complete. If the consumer crashes or takes longer than the timeout, the message reappears in the queue and another consumer can pick it up. This guarantees at-least-once delivery.

What is a dead-letter queue and when do messages end up there?:::A DLQ is a separate SQS queue that receives messages that failed processing more than maxReceiveCount times. After N failed attempts (message reappears N times without being deleted), SQS moves the message to the DLQ. You then inspect and diagnose why those messages failed without losing them.

Describe the fanout pattern using SNS and SQS.:::A single SNS topic has multiple SQS queues as subscribers. When a publisher sends one message to the SNS topic, SNS delivers a copy to every subscribed queue simultaneously. Each queue is independently processed by its own consumer service. This decouples the publisher from knowing how many consumers exist.

When would you choose Kinesis Data Streams over SQS?:::Choose Kinesis when multiple independent consumers need to read the same data stream (e.g., a real-time dashboard AND a machine learning pipeline both consuming the same click-stream events). Kinesis is also better when you need to replay historical events or maintain strict per-shard ordering at high throughput. SQS is better for simple work-queue patterns where each task must be processed exactly once by one worker.

What is EventBridge and how does it differ from SNS?:::EventBridge is a serverless event bus that routes events using JSON content-based filtering rules. Unlike SNS (which is push-based to pre-configured subscribers), EventBridge supports sophisticated pattern matching on event content, built-in integration with 200+ AWS services as event sources, schema registry, and event replay. EventBridge is preferred for building loosely coupled event-driven architectures across services.
sessions.successCriteria: You can explain Standard vs FIFO trade-offs, draw a fanout architecture with SNS+SQS, configure a DLQ with redrive policy, and articulate when to use Kinesis vs SQS and EventBridge vs SNS.

sessions.title: Containers: ECS and EKS
sessions.paretoJustification: Containers are the dominant deployment unit in AWS. ECS is used at companies of all sizes; EKS is standard for teams already on Kubernetes. Both appear heavily in AWS Solutions Architect and DevOps interviews.
objectives: Write an ECS task definition with container definitions, CPU/memory limits, and environment variables
Understand the difference between Fargate and EC2 launch types
Create an ECS service with desired count, load balancer integration, and auto-scaling
Understand EKS cluster components: control plane, node groups, pods, and services
Use ECR to build, tag, push, and pull container images

activities.description: Build a Docker image, push it to ECR, and verify the image appears in the repository
Register an ECS task definition via CLI and launch a standalone Fargate task
Create an ECS service with 2 desired tasks behind an Application Load Balancer
Review EKS core concepts: kubectl commands, deployment YAML, service types (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)

sessions.content:
## ECR: Elastic Container Registry

ECR is a fully managed Docker-compatible container registry. Each AWS account gets a private registry; you create repositories per image.

```bash
# Authenticate Docker to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
  docker login --username AWS \
  --password-stdin 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Create a repository
aws ecr create-repository --repository-name my-api

# Build, tag, and push
docker build -t my-api .
docker tag my-api:latest \
  123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest
docker push \
  123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest
```

## ECS: Elastic Container Service

### Core Concepts

- **Cluster**: Logical grouping of tasks/services (Fargate or EC2)
- **Task Definition**: Blueprint — image, CPU, memory, ports, env vars, IAM role
- **Task**: Running instance of a task definition (analogous to a pod)
- **Service**: Maintains N running tasks, integrates with ALB, handles rolling updates

### Task Definition

```bash
# task-definition.json
cat > task-definition.json << 'EOF'
{
  "family": "my-api",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "256",
  "memory": "512",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "my-api",
      "image": "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest",
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8080, "protocol": "tcp"}],
      "environment": [
        {"name": "ENV", "value": "production"},
        {"name": "PORT", "value": "8080"}
      ],
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/my-api",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      },
      "essential": true
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Register the task definition
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --cli-input-json file://task-definition.json
```

### Fargate vs EC2 Launch Type

| Aspect | Fargate | EC2 |
|---|---|---|
| Server management | None — AWS manages | You manage EC2 instances |
| Pricing | Per vCPU/memory-second | Per EC2 instance-hour |
| Density control | None | Full control |
| Use case | Most workloads, simplicity | GPU workloads, custom AMIs, cost optimization at scale |

### ECS Service

```bash
# Create an ECS cluster
aws ecs create-cluster --cluster-name production

# Create a service with 2 tasks
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster production \
  --service-name my-api-service \
  --task-definition my-api:1 \
  --desired-count 2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration \
    "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123,subnet-def456],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}"

# Update service (rolling deployment)
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster production \
  --service my-api-service \
  --task-definition my-api:2

# Check deployment status
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster production \
  --services my-api-service \
  --query 'services[0].deployments'
```

## EKS: Elastic Kubernetes Service

EKS is a managed Kubernetes control plane. AWS runs etcd and the API server; you manage worker node groups (EC2 or Fargate).

```bash
# Create cluster (takes ~15 min)
eksctl create cluster \
  --name my-cluster \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --nodegroup-name workers \
  --node-type t3.medium \
  --nodes 3

# Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --name my-cluster

# Deploy an application
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl get pods -n default
kubectl get services
```

### Kubernetes Deployment YAML (for reference)

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-api
        image: 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "250m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
```

### ECS vs EKS Choice

- **ECS**: Simpler, AWS-native, less operational overhead. Good for teams not already using Kubernetes.
- **EKS**: Full Kubernetes API compatibility, better for multi-cloud portability, advanced scheduling, or existing K8s expertise.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Containers: ECS and EKS — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Containers: ECS and EKS ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Containers: ECS and EKS");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

```python
from typing import Dict, List, Any

def demonstrate():
    """Containers: ECS and EKS — Key concepts demonstration"""
    print(f"=== Containers: ECS and EKS ===")

    # Core data structures
    config: Dict[str, Any] = {
        'topic': 'Containers: ECS and EKS',
        'complexity': 'O(n)',
        'space': 'O(1)',
    }
    for k, v in config.items():
        print(f'{k}: {v}')

    # Typical operations
    data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(f'Input: {data}')
    print(f'Size: {len(data)}')
    print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    demonstrate()
```
reviewQuestions: What is the difference between an ECS task and an ECS service?:::A task is a single running instance of a task definition (like a single pod). A service is a long-running construct that ensures N tasks are always running, restarts failed tasks, integrates with load balancers, and orchestrates rolling deployments when the task definition is updated.

Compare Fargate and EC2 launch types for ECS.:::Fargate is serverless — AWS manages the underlying infrastructure. You specify CPU and memory per task and pay per second of usage. No EC2 instances to patch or right-size. EC2 launch type requires you to manage the EC2 instances in the cluster but gives more control (custom AMIs, GPU support, reserved instance pricing, bin-packing multiple tasks per host for cost efficiency at scale).

What does an ECS task definition contain?:::A task definition is a JSON blueprint specifying: container image(s) and version, CPU and memory allocation, port mappings, environment variables, secrets references (from Secrets Manager or SSM), IAM execution role, logging configuration (e.g., CloudWatch Logs), and network mode (awsvpc is required for Fargate).

What is ECR and why would you use it instead of Docker Hub?:::ECR is AWS's managed container registry. Benefits over Docker Hub: images are stored in your own AWS account (private by default), IAM controls access, images can be pulled from ECS/EKS without extra credentials (using execution role), vulnerability scanning is built in, and there is no egress cost between ECR and ECS/EKS in the same region.

What are the main Kubernetes resource types you need to know for EKS?:::Pod (smallest deployable unit, one or more containers), Deployment (manages replica sets, rolling updates), Service (stable network endpoint for pods — ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer), ConfigMap (non-sensitive config), Secret (sensitive config), Ingress (HTTP routing rules), and Namespace (logical isolation). On EKS, a LoadBalancer service type automatically provisions an AWS ELB.

How does ECS handle rolling deployments?:::When you update a service with a new task definition revision, ECS starts new tasks with the new version and, once healthy (passing load balancer health checks), terminates old tasks. You control the pace with minimumHealthyPercent (default 100) and maximumPercent (default 200). A 100/200 setting means ECS can double capacity during deployment and will not go below current desired count.
sessions.successCriteria: You can write an ECS task definition, explain Fargate vs EC2 trade-offs, describe how ECS services maintain availability during deployments, and know when to choose ECS vs EKS.

sessions.title: DevOps: CloudFormation and CI/CD Pipelines
sessions.paretoJustification: Infrastructure as Code and CI/CD pipelines are core DevOps competencies expected in any AWS role. CloudFormation and the Code* suite are the native AWS stack and appear in every DevOps interview.
objectives: Write a CloudFormation template with parameters, resources, and outputs
Understand stacks, change sets, and stack rollback behavior
Know the AWS CDK workflow (synth, diff, deploy) and how it generates CloudFormation
Build a CodePipeline with Source (CodeCommit/GitHub), Build (CodeBuild), and Deploy (CodeDeploy) stages
Configure a CodeBuild buildspec.yml for a Node.js or Java build

activities.description: Write a CloudFormation template that provisions an S3 bucket with versioning and a lifecycle rule, deploy with change sets
Create a CodeBuild project with a buildspec.yml that runs tests and produces an artifact
Assemble a three-stage CodePipeline (Source → Build → Deploy) and trigger it by pushing code
Explore AWS CDK: initialize a project, define an S3 bucket in TypeScript, run cdk synth to view generated CloudFormation

sessions.content:
## CloudFormation

CloudFormation provisions and manages AWS resources using declarative JSON or YAML templates. The collection of resources deployed together is called a **stack**.

### Template Anatomy

```yaml
# cloudformation/api-stack.yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: API service stack

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, staging, prod]
    Default: dev
  InstanceType:
    Type: String
    Default: t3.micro

Resources:
  # S3 bucket for artifacts
  ArtifactBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: !Sub 'artifacts-${Environment}-${AWS::AccountId}'
      VersioningConfiguration:
        Status: Enabled
      LifecycleConfiguration:
        Rules:
          - Id: DeleteOldVersions
            Status: Enabled
            NoncurrentVersionExpirationInDays: 30

  # DynamoDB table
  OrdersTable:
    Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
    Properties:
      TableName: !Sub 'orders-${Environment}'
      BillingMode: PAY_PER_REQUEST
      AttributeDefinitions:
        - AttributeName: orderId
          AttributeType: S
      KeySchema:
        - AttributeName: orderId
          KeyType: HASH

Outputs:
  BucketName:
    Value: !Ref ArtifactBucket
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-BucketName'
  TableArn:
    Value: !GetAtt OrdersTable.Arn
```

### CLI Operations

```bash
# Validate template syntax
aws cloudformation validate-template \
  --template-body file://api-stack.yaml

# Create a change set (preview before deploying)
aws cloudformation create-change-set \
  --stack-name api-production \
  --template-body file://api-stack.yaml \
  --parameters ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=prod \
  --change-set-name update-v2

# Review the change set
aws cloudformation describe-change-set \
  --stack-name api-production \
  --change-set-name update-v2

# Execute the change set
aws cloudformation execute-change-set \
  --stack-name api-production \
  --change-set-name update-v2

# Deploy shorthand (create or update)
aws cloudformation deploy \
  --template-file api-stack.yaml \
  --stack-name api-production \
  --parameter-overrides Environment=prod \
  --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM

# Delete a stack
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name api-production
```

### Stack Rollback

If any resource creation fails during a deploy, CloudFormation automatically rolls back the entire stack to its last known good state. You can disable rollback for debugging: `--on-failure DO_NOTHING`.

## AWS CDK

CDK lets you define infrastructure in TypeScript, Python, Java, or Go. `cdk synth` compiles your code to a CloudFormation template.

```typescript
// lib/my-stack.ts
import * as cdk from 'aws-cdk-lib';
import * as s3 from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';
import * as lambda from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-lambda';

export class MyStack extends cdk.Stack {
  constructor(scope: cdk.App, id: string, props?: cdk.StackProps) {
    super(scope, id, props);

    const bucket = new s3.Bucket(this, 'ArtifactBucket', {
      versioned: true,
      removalPolicy: cdk.RemovalPolicy.DESTROY,
      lifecycleRules: [{
        noncurrentVersionExpiration: cdk.Duration.days(30),
      }],
    });

    const fn = new lambda.Function(this, 'ProcessorFn', {
      runtime: lambda.Runtime.NODEJS_20_X,
      handler: 'index.handler',
      code: lambda.Code.fromAsset('lambda'),
      environment: { BUCKET: bucket.bucketName },
    });

    bucket.grantReadWrite(fn); // Adds IAM policy automatically
  }
}
```

```bash
npx cdk synth    # Generate CloudFormation template
npx cdk diff     # Show what will change
npx cdk deploy   # Deploy the stack
npx cdk destroy  # Delete the stack
```

## CodePipeline + CodeBuild + CodeDeploy

### buildspec.yml (CodeBuild)

```yaml
# buildspec.yml — placed at root of repository
version: 0.2

phases:
  install:
    runtime-versions:
      nodejs: 20
    commands:
      - npm ci
  pre_build:
    commands:
      - echo Running tests...
      - npm test
  build:
    commands:
      - npm run build
      - echo Build completed
  post_build:
    commands:
      - echo Packaging artifact...

artifacts:
  files:
    - '**/*'
  base-directory: dist

cache:
  paths:
    - node_modules/**/*
```

### Create a CodePipeline via CLI

```bash
# Create CodeBuild project
aws codebuild create-project \
  --name my-api-build \
  --source '{"type": "CODEPIPELINE"}' \
  --artifacts '{"type": "CODEPIPELINE"}' \
  --environment '{"type": "LINUX_CONTAINER", "computeType": "BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL", "image": "aws/codebuild/standard:7.0"}' \
  --service-role arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/CodeBuildRole

# Pipelines are most easily created via CloudFormation or CDK
# but the pipeline structure has 3 stages:
# Stage 1 — Source: GitHub/CodeCommit triggers on push
# Stage 2 — Build: CodeBuild runs buildspec.yml
# Stage 3 — Deploy: CodeDeploy pushes to ECS/EC2/Lambda
```

**CodeDeploy deployment strategies:**
- **AllAtOnce**: Fastest, zero capacity during deployment
- **HalfAtATime**: 50% capacity maintained
- **OneAtATime**: Slowest, safest (zero downtime)
- **Canary10Percent5Minutes**: Shift 10% traffic, wait 5 min, then 100%

reviewQuestions: What is a CloudFormation change set and why use one?:::A change set is a preview of the changes CloudFormation will make to a running stack before you execute them. You create a change set, review which resources will be Added, Modified, or Removed, and only then execute it. This prevents accidental destructive changes, especially on production stacks where a resource replacement would cause downtime or data loss.

What does CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM mean in CloudFormation deployments?:::CloudFormation requires explicit acknowledgment when a template creates or modifies IAM resources (roles, policies) that are named. Passing --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM tells CloudFormation you understand the security implications and consent to creating named IAM resources. Without it, the deploy fails with InsufficientCapabilitiesException.

Explain the AWS CDK workflow from code to deployed AWS resources.:::You write infrastructure in a typed language (e.g., TypeScript). `cdk synth` compiles it to a CloudFormation template and outputs it to the `cdk.out/` directory. `cdk diff` compares the synthesized template against the currently deployed stack and shows what will change. `cdk deploy` uploads the template and calls CloudFormation to apply the changes. CDK also handles asset bundling (e.g., Lambda zip files) automatically.

What is a buildspec.yml and what phases does CodeBuild execute?:::buildspec.yml is a YAML file at the repository root (or specified separately) that tells CodeBuild how to build your project. Phases are: install (install runtimes/tools), pre_build (pre-build steps like login to ECR, run linters), build (the actual compilation and testing), post_build (packaging, tagging). The artifacts section specifies which files to pass to the next pipeline stage.

What is the difference between CodeBuild and CodeDeploy?:::CodeBuild is a build and test service — it compiles source code, runs tests, and produces artifacts (e.g., a Docker image or a zip file). CodeDeploy is a deployment service — it takes the built artifact and deploys it to target compute (EC2 instances, ECS services, Lambda functions). They are separate concerns deliberately: you could use GitHub Actions for build and CodeDeploy for deployment.

How does CloudFormation handle rollback on deployment failure?:::If a resource fails to create during a stack create or update, CloudFormation rolls back all changes in that operation to restore the stack to its previous stable state. For a new stack creation failure, the stack is deleted entirely. You can inspect the Events tab in the console to see the specific resource and error that caused the failure. Using --on-failure DO_NOTHING during a create lets you inspect the partial state for debugging.
sessions.successCriteria: You can write a valid CloudFormation template with parameters and outputs, explain change sets, describe the CDK synth-diff-deploy workflow, and assemble a CodePipeline with proper buildspec.yml phases.

sessions.title: Security: KMS, WAF, Shield, and IAM Deep Dive
sessions.paretoJustification: Security is non-negotiable in AWS. KMS, IAM policies, WAF, and GuardDuty are the most commonly tested security services in AWS certifications and interviews, and understanding the shared responsibility model is foundational.
objectives: Manage KMS keys (CMK creation, key rotation, key policies vs. IAM policies)
Use Secrets Manager to store and auto-rotate database credentials
Write IAM policies with least-privilege using condition keys
Configure WAF rules to block SQL injection and rate-limit by IP
Understand Shield Standard vs. Advanced and GuardDuty threat detection

activities.description: Create a KMS CMK, encrypt/decrypt a file, and inspect the key policy to understand who can administer vs. use the key
Store a database password in Secrets Manager, retrieve it via CLI, and observe automatic rotation configuration
Write an IAM policy that allows S3 read access only from a specific VPC endpoint, test with aws iam simulate-principal-policy
Create a WAF Web ACL with AWS Managed Rules (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) and attach it to an ALB

sessions.content:
## KMS: Key Management Service

KMS manages encryption keys. Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) give you full control: key policy, rotation, and audit via CloudTrail.

```bash
# Create a symmetric CMK
aws kms create-key \
  --description "Production data encryption key" \
  --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT \
  --origin AWS_KMS

# Create a human-readable alias
aws kms create-alias \
  --alias-name alias/prod-data-key \
  --target-key-id <key-id>

# Encrypt a file
aws kms encrypt \
  --key-id alias/prod-data-key \
  --plaintext fileb://secret.txt \
  --query CiphertextBlob \
  --output text | base64 --decode > secret.enc

# Decrypt
aws kms decrypt \
  --ciphertext-blob fileb://secret.enc \
  --query Plaintext \
  --output text | base64 --decode

# Enable automatic annual key rotation
aws kms enable-key-rotation --key-id alias/prod-data-key
```

### Key Policy vs IAM Policy

KMS uses a dual-authorization model. **Both** must allow the action:
- **Key policy** (resource-based): controls who can administer and use the key
- **IAM policy** (identity-based): the user/role must also have kms:Decrypt in their IAM policy

If the key policy grants access to root (`arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:root`), then IAM policies in that account can grant key usage. If the key policy restricts to specific principals, those restrictions override IAM policies.

## Secrets Manager

Secrets Manager stores credentials, API keys, and tokens with built-in rotation.

```bash
# Store a database secret
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
  --name prod/myapp/db-password \
  --description "RDS master password" \
  --secret-string '{"username":"admin","password":"s3cr3t!"}'

# Retrieve at runtime (in app startup)
SECRET=$(aws secretsmanager get-secret-value \
  --secret-id prod/myapp/db-password \
  --query SecretString --output text)
DB_PASS=$(echo $SECRET | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['password'])")

# Enable auto-rotation (Lambda rotator for RDS)
aws secretsmanager rotate-secret \
  --secret-id prod/myapp/db-password \
  --rotation-lambda-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:function:SecretsManagerRDSRotator \
  --rotation-rules AutomaticallyAfterDays=30
```

## IAM: Least-Privilege Policies

```json
// Least-privilege: S3 read only from specific VPC endpoint
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-secure-bucket",
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-secure-bucket/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "aws:SourceVpce": "vpce-0123456789abcdef0"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

```bash
# Simulate policy evaluation
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/MyAppRole \
  --action-names s3:GetObject \
  --resource-arns arn:aws:s3:::my-secure-bucket/data.csv

# Common IAM best practices
# 1. Never use root account credentials
# 2. Require MFA for console access
# 3. Use roles for EC2/Lambda (not access keys on instances)
# 4. Use IAM Access Analyzer to find overly permissive policies
aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer \
  --analyzer-name prod-analyzer \
  --type ACCOUNT
```

## WAF: Web Application Firewall

WAF sits in front of ALB, CloudFront, or API Gateway. It evaluates HTTP requests against rules and either allows or blocks them.

```bash
# Create a WAF Web ACL
aws wafv2 create-web-acl \
  --name my-web-acl \
  --scope REGIONAL \
  --default-action Allow={} \
  --rules '[
    {
      "Name": "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet",
      "Priority": 1,
      "OverrideAction": {"None": {}},
      "Statement": {
        "ManagedRuleGroupStatement": {
          "VendorName": "AWS",
          "Name": "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet"
        }
      },
      "VisibilityConfig": {
        "SampledRequestsEnabled": true,
        "CloudWatchMetricsEnabled": true,
        "MetricName": "CommonRuleSetMetric"
      }
    }
  ]' \
  --visibility-config SampledRequestsEnabled=true,CloudWatchMetricsEnabled=true,MetricName=my-web-acl

# Associate Web ACL with ALB
aws wafv2 associate-web-acl \
  --web-acl-arn arn:aws:wafv2:us-east-1:123456789:regional/webacl/my-web-acl/xxx \
  --resource-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789:loadbalancer/app/my-alb/xxx
```

## Shield and GuardDuty

**Shield Standard** (free, automatic): protects all AWS resources against common layer 3/4 DDoS attacks (SYN floods, UDP reflection).

**Shield Advanced** ($3,000/month): adds layer 7 DDoS protection, 24/7 DRT (DDoS Response Team) access, financial protection (cost credits during attack), and real-time attack visibility.

**GuardDuty**: ML-based threat detection. Analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to detect unusual API calls, compromised IAM credentials, crypto-mining, and port scanning.

```bash
# Enable GuardDuty
aws guardduty create-detector --enable

# List findings
aws guardduty list-findings \
  --detector-id <detector-id> \
  --finding-criteria '{"Criterion":{"severity":{"Gte":7}}}'
```

### Java Implementation

```java

public class Main {
    // Security: KMS, WAF, Shield, and IAM Deep Dive — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Security: KMS, WAF, Shield, and IAM Deep Dive ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic

        config.put("topic", "Security: KMS, WAF, Shield, and IAM Deep Dive");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");

        }

        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a KMS key policy and an IAM policy, and which takes precedence?:::Both must grant the action for it to succeed — KMS uses a dual-authorization model. The key policy is a resource-based policy attached to the KMS key. The IAM policy is attached to the identity (user/role). If the key policy grants kms:Decrypt to the AWS account root, then IAM policies in that account can grant usage. If the key policy restricts access to specific principals only, those restrictions cannot be overridden by IAM policies.

Why should you use Secrets Manager instead of environment variables for database passwords?:::Environment variables are visible in process listings, crash dumps, and logs. They do not rotate automatically and require redeployment to change. Secrets Manager stores secrets encrypted (using KMS), provides fine-grained IAM access control, supports automatic rotation (a Lambda function rotates the secret and updates the database), and provides an audit trail via CloudTrail of every access. Applications retrieve the current secret at runtime without a redeploy.

What are IAM condition keys and give a real-world example?:::Condition keys add context-based restrictions to IAM policy statements. Examples: aws:SourceIp (restrict to specific IP ranges), aws:SourceVpce (restrict to a VPC endpoint), aws:RequestedRegion (restrict to specific regions), aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent (require MFA), s3:prefix (restrict S3 access to specific prefixes). Real example: deny all S3 PutObject unless aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is true — protecting against accidental or malicious writes.

What does AWS WAF protect against that Shield does not?:::Shield protects against network-layer DDoS (layer 3/4) — volumetric attacks like SYN floods that try to overwhelm network capacity. WAF protects at layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) — it inspects HTTP request content to block SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), bad bots, malicious user agents, and rate-based attacks (too many requests from one IP). Shield Advanced includes some layer 7 protection but WAF provides the detailed rule-based control.

How does GuardDuty detect threats without installing agents?:::GuardDuty is agentless. It ingests and analyzes three data sources already available in AWS: AWS CloudTrail event logs (API calls made in your account), VPC Flow Logs (network traffic metadata — who talked to whom, port, bytes), and DNS query logs (DNS resolutions made by EC2 instances). It applies machine learning and threat intelligence to detect anomalies like API calls from known malicious IPs, unusual IAM usage patterns, or DNS queries to known command-and-control domains.

What is the AWS Shared Responsibility Model?:::AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud — physical hardware, host OS, hypervisor, network infrastructure, and the managed service itself. The customer is responsible for security IN the cloud — the guest OS on EC2, application code, IAM permissions, data encryption (at rest and in transit), security group rules, and network ACL configuration. For managed services like RDS, AWS handles the database engine patching; you handle encryption and access control.
sessions.successCriteria: You can explain the KMS dual-authorization model, describe Secrets Manager's rotation mechanism, write a least-privilege IAM policy with condition keys, and articulate the difference between WAF, Shield, and GuardDuty.

sessions.title: Architecture: Well-Architected Framework and Patterns
sessions.paretoJustification: AWS Well-Architected Framework is the lens through which all AWS architecture decisions are evaluated. The 6 pillars directly map to interview questions on reliability, performance, cost, and security trade-offs.
objectives: Name and explain all 6 pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework
Design a multi-tier (presentation/application/data) architecture on AWS
Understand microservices decomposition patterns and how they map to AWS services
Define RTO and RPO and design disaster recovery strategies (Backup/Restore, Pilot Light, Warm Standby, Multi-Site Active-Active)
Apply the Well-Architected Framework to critique a given architecture

activities.description: Walk through each of the 6 pillars and write one concrete AWS decision that exemplifies it
Draw a multi-tier AWS architecture for an e-commerce application (Route 53 → CloudFront → ALB → ECS → RDS + ElastiCache + S3)
Compare the four DR strategies by filling in a table of RTO, RPO, cost, and AWS services used for each
Identify Well-Architected violations in a given single-AZ, single-instance, no-backup architecture and list remediations

sessions.content:
## The 6 Pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework

### 1. Operational Excellence
Running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and continually improving processes.

- Perform operations as code (CloudFormation, CDK, Ansible)
- Make frequent, small, reversible changes (CI/CD pipelines)
- Anticipate failure: game days, chaos engineering (AWS Fault Injection Simulator)
- Learn from operational events: post-mortems, runbooks in AWS Systems Manager

### 2. Security
Protecting data, systems, and assets while delivering business value.

- Implement a strong identity foundation: IAM roles with least-privilege, no long-lived keys
- Enable traceability: CloudTrail, Config, VPC Flow Logs
- Apply security at all layers: VPC, SG, NACL, WAF, encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS)
- Automate security best practices: AWS Config rules, Security Hub

### 3. Reliability
The ability to recover from failures and dynamically acquire resources to meet demand.

- Design for failure: multi-AZ RDS, ECS across multiple AZs, S3 cross-region replication
- Use managed services: they handle underlying infrastructure failures
- Implement health checks and auto-healing: ECS service restarts failed tasks
- Test recovery procedures: actually run DR drills, not just plan them

### 4. Performance Efficiency
Using computing resources efficiently to meet requirements as demand changes.

- Select the right resource type: Graviton3 EC2 for 40% better price/performance for most workloads
- Use caching: ElastiCache Redis for session data, CloudFront for static assets
- Use serverless: Lambda eliminates over-provisioning entirely
- Monitor performance: CloudWatch metrics, X-Ray distributed tracing

### 5. Cost Optimization
Avoiding unnecessary costs and spending only on what is needed.

- Right-size resources: use Compute Optimizer recommendations
- Use appropriate pricing models: Savings Plans for predictable workloads, Spot for fault-tolerant batch
- Implement lifecycle policies: S3 Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier for archival
- Use serverless to eliminate idle capacity

### 6. Sustainability (added 2021)
Minimizing the environmental impact of cloud workloads.

- Maximize utilization: run fewer, larger instances at higher utilization vs. many small ones
- Use managed services: AWS manages energy-efficient hardware refresh cycles
- Use Graviton (Arm-based): 60% better energy efficiency than x86 equivalents
- Reduce data transfer: cache at edge with CloudFront, minimize cross-region traffic

## Multi-Tier AWS Architecture

```
Internet
    |
Route 53 (DNS + health checks)
    |
CloudFront (CDN — static assets, edge caching)
    |
ALB (Application Load Balancer — multi-AZ)
    |
ECS Fargate (Application tier — stateless, auto-scaled)
    |           |
    |     ElastiCache Redis (Session + query cache)
    |
RDS Aurora (Multi-AZ — write primary + read replica)
    |
S3 (Object storage — user uploads, static files)
```

**Key design decisions:**
- CloudFront serves static assets from edge caches, reducing ALB load
- ALB routes across multiple AZs; ECS auto-scales on CPU/memory
- Aurora Multi-AZ provides automatic failover in ~30 seconds
- ElastiCache reduces read load on Aurora by 80%+ for repeated queries

## Microservices Patterns on AWS

**Strangler Fig Pattern**: Gradually replace a monolith by routing specific endpoints to new microservices via ALB path-based routing, leaving the rest on the monolith until fully migrated.

**Saga Pattern**: For distributed transactions across microservices. Each service publishes events (via SQS/SNS/EventBridge); compensating transactions roll back failed steps.

**CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)**: Separate write operations (DynamoDB as write store) from read operations (OpenSearch or ElastiCache as read-optimized projections).

## Disaster Recovery Strategies

| Strategy | RTO | RPO | Cost | AWS Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Backup & Restore | Hours | Hours | Lowest | S3 backups + CloudFormation to rebuild |
| Pilot Light | 10–30 min | Minutes | Low | Minimal core infra in DR region, scale on failover |
| Warm Standby | Minutes | Seconds | Medium | Scaled-down but running replica in DR region |
| Multi-Site Active-Active | Near-zero | Near-zero | Highest | Full capacity in both regions, Route 53 routing |

**RTO (Recovery Time Objective)**: Maximum acceptable time for the system to be unavailable after a failure.

**RPO (Recovery Point Objective)**: Maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time.

```bash
# Test RDS failover (simulates AZ failure)
aws rds failover-db-cluster \
  --db-cluster-identifier my-aurora-cluster

# Route 53 health-check based failover
aws route53 create-health-check \
  --caller-reference 2024-01-01 \
  --health-check-config \
    'IPAddress=203.0.113.1,Port=443,Type=HTTPS,ResourcePath=/health,FullyQualifiedDomainName=api.myapp.com,RequestInterval=30,FailureThreshold=3'
```

### Java Implementation

```java
public class Main {
    // Architecture: Well-Architected Framework and Patterns — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Architecture: Well-Architected Framework and Patterns ===");

        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

reviewQuestions: Name the 6 pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework.:::Operational Excellence (run and improve operations), Security (protect data and systems), Reliability (recover from failures and scale), Performance Efficiency (use resources efficiently), Cost Optimization (avoid unnecessary costs), and Sustainability (minimize environmental impact). The first five existed from the start; Sustainability was added in 2021.

What is the difference between RTO and RPO?:::RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum acceptable downtime — how long the business can tolerate the system being unavailable before the impact is unacceptable. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is the maximum acceptable data loss — how old can the recovered data be. A hospital might have RPO = 0 (zero data loss) and RTO = 15 minutes. A blog might accept RPO = 24 hours and RTO = 4 hours.

Describe the Pilot Light disaster recovery strategy.:::Pilot Light keeps a minimal, always-on version of the critical core infrastructure running in the DR region (the 'pilot light' — just enough to ignite quickly). For example, RDS is replicated to the DR region but EC2 and ECS are not running. On failover, you point DNS to the DR region, scale up ECS, and restore from the replicated RDS. RTO is 10–30 minutes. It is much cheaper than Warm Standby or Active-Active.

How does multi-AZ deployment address reliability?:::Spreading resources across multiple Availability Zones means a single AZ failure (which can happen due to power, cooling, or network failures) does not take down the application. ALB routes to healthy ECS tasks in other AZs. RDS Aurora promotes a read replica in another AZ as the new primary. Each AZ is a physically separate data center with independent power and networking within the same region.

What is the Strangler Fig pattern and when would you use it?:::The Strangler Fig pattern is a microservices migration strategy for breaking apart a monolith incrementally. You place a proxy (e.g., ALB or API Gateway) in front of the monolith. For each feature you want to extract, you build a new microservice, then reroute that traffic path to the new service. The monolith gradually shrinks ('strangled') until all routes are migrated and the monolith is decommissioned. This is safer than a 'big bang' rewrite.

How does CloudFront improve both performance and cost in a multi-tier architecture?:::CloudFront caches content at edge locations close to users, reducing latency for static assets (images, JS, CSS) from the origin server's region-level latency to sub-10ms edge latency. This also reduces origin load — the ALB and ECS tasks see far fewer requests for cacheable content. CloudFront also absorbs DDoS attacks (with Shield) before they reach the ALB, and HTTPS termination at the edge reduces TLS overhead on origin servers.
sessions.successCriteria: You can name all 6 pillars with examples, draw a multi-tier AWS architecture with correct service placement, define RTO and RPO, and compare the four DR strategies by cost and recovery time.

sessions.title: Cost Optimization and AWS Best Practices
sessions.paretoJustification: Cost optimization is a top priority for every engineering team using AWS. Interviewers at senior levels regularly ask how you have reduced cloud costs. Understanding pricing models, rightsizing, and tagging is essential.
objectives: Explain On-Demand, Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, and Spot Instance pricing models
Right-size EC2 instances using AWS Compute Optimizer
Implement S3 cost optimization with lifecycle policies and Intelligent-Tiering
Use cost allocation tags to attribute spending to teams and projects
Read and interpret an AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR)

activities.description: Use the AWS Pricing Calculator to estimate the monthly cost of a 3-tier web application (EC2, RDS, ALB, CloudFront)
Enable Compute Optimizer for the account and review rightsizing recommendations for EC2 instances
Create an S3 lifecycle policy that transitions objects to Infrequent Access after 30 days and Glacier after 90 days
Set up a billing alarm in CloudWatch and enable Cost Anomaly Detection for the account

sessions.content:
## AWS Pricing Models

### On-Demand
Pay per second (Linux EC2) or per hour (Windows), no commitment. Most expensive per unit but no upfront cost. Use for unpredictable or short-lived workloads.

### Reserved Instances (RI)
1-year or 3-year commitment. Up to 72% savings vs. On-Demand.
- **Standard RI**: locked to specific instance type/AZ. Greatest discount.
- **Convertible RI**: can change instance family during term. ~54% savings.
- Payment options: All Upfront (deepest discount), Partial Upfront, No Upfront.

### Savings Plans
More flexible than RIs. Commit to a $/hour spend (e.g., $0.10/hr) for 1 or 3 years.
- **Compute Savings Plans**: apply to EC2 (any family, region, OS), Fargate, and Lambda. ~66% savings.
- **EC2 Instance Savings Plans**: apply to a specific EC2 instance family in a region. ~72% savings.
- **SageMaker Savings Plans**: for SageMaker usage.

**Savings Plans vs RI**: Savings Plans are generally preferred because they are simpler and apply automatically to matching usage — no AZ or instance-type lock-in.

### Spot Instances
Bid on unused EC2 capacity. Up to 90% savings vs. On-Demand. **Caveat**: AWS can reclaim Spot instances with 2-minute notice when capacity is needed.

Use Spot for: batch processing, CI/CD build agents, EMR data processing, fault-tolerant ML training.

```bash
# Request a Spot instance
aws ec2 request-spot-instances \
  --instance-count 1 \
  --type one-time \
  --launch-specification '{
    "ImageId": "ami-0abcdef1234567890",
    "InstanceType": "m5.large",
    "KeyName": "my-key",
    "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-abc123"]
  }' \
  --spot-price "0.05"  # max price per hour

# Use Spot with Auto Scaling (preferred — handles interruptions)
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name my-asg \
  --mixed-instances-policy '{
    "InstancesDistribution": {
      "OnDemandPercentageAboveBaseCapacity": 20,
      "SpotAllocationStrategy": "capacity-optimized"
    }
  }' \
  --min-size 2 --max-size 20 --desired-capacity 5
```

## Rightsizing with Compute Optimizer

```bash
# Enable Compute Optimizer
aws compute-optimizer update-enrollment-status --status Active

# Get EC2 recommendations
aws compute-optimizer get-ec2-instance-recommendations \
  --query 'instanceRecommendations[].{
    Instance: instanceArn,
    Current: currentInstanceType,
    Recommended: recommendationOptions[0].instanceType,
    Savings: recommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.value
  }' \
  --output table
```

Compute Optimizer analyzes 14 days of CloudWatch metrics (CPU, memory, network, disk) to suggest downsizing over-provisioned instances or upgrading undersized ones.

## S3 Cost Optimization

S3 storage tiers by cost (highest to lowest):
1. **S3 Standard**: $0.023/GB — frequently accessed
2. **S3 Standard-IA**: $0.0125/GB — infrequent access, min 30-day retention
3. **S3 One Zone-IA**: $0.01/GB — non-critical, single AZ
4. **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval**: $0.004/GB — archive, millisecond retrieval
5. **S3 Glacier Deep Archive**: $0.00099/GB — long-term archive, 12h retrieval

```bash
# Create lifecycle policy: IA after 30 days, Glacier after 90 days
aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration \
  --bucket my-data-bucket \
  --lifecycle-configuration '{
    "Rules": [{
      "Id": "ArchiveOldData",
      "Status": "Enabled",
      "Filter": {"Prefix": "logs/"},
      "Transitions": [
        {"Days": 30, "StorageClass": "STANDARD_IA"},
        {"Days": 90, "StorageClass": "GLACIER"}
      ],
      "Expiration": {"Days": 365}
    }]
  }'

# Enable Intelligent-Tiering (automatic tiering based on access patterns)
aws s3api put-bucket-intelligent-tiering-configuration \
  --bucket my-data-bucket \
  --id EntireBucket \
  --intelligent-tiering-configuration '{
    "Id": "EntireBucket",
    "Status": "Enabled",
    "Tierings": [
      {"Days": 90, "AccessTier": "ARCHIVE_ACCESS"},
      {"Days": 180, "AccessTier": "DEEP_ARCHIVE_ACCESS"}
    ]
  }'
```

## Cost Allocation Tags and Governance

```bash
# Tag resources for cost attribution
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources i-1234567890abcdef0 \
  --tags Key=Project,Value=checkout-service \
         Key=Team,Value=payments \
         Key=Environment,Value=production \
         Key=CostCenter,Value=CC-1042

# Activate user-defined cost allocation tags in Billing console
# (done via console or API — required before tags appear in CUR)
aws ce update-cost-allocation-tags-status \
  --cost-allocation-tags-status '[{"TagKey": "Project", "Status": "Active"}]'

# Set a billing alarm
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name MonthlyBudgetAlert \
  --metric-name EstimatedCharges \
  --namespace AWS/Billing \
  --statistic Maximum \
  --period 86400 \
  --threshold 500 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789:billing-alerts \
  --dimensions Name=Currency,Value=USD

# Enable Cost Anomaly Detection
aws ce create-anomaly-monitor \
  --anomaly-monitor '{
    "MonitorName": "MyMonitor",
    "MonitorType": "DIMENSIONAL",
    "MonitorDimension": "SERVICE"
  }'
```

## Cost Optimization Summary Matrix

| Workload | Recommended Model | Savings |
|---|---|---|
| 24/7 steady-state (web servers) | Savings Plans (1-year Compute) | ~40-66% |
| Batch jobs, CI/CD | Spot Instances + On-Demand base | ~60-90% |
| Databases | Reserved Instances (1-year) | ~40-60% |
| Serverless APIs | Lambda On-Demand (no RI needed) | Already optimized |
| Dev/Test environments | Schedule-based stop/start | ~65% (12h/day) |

**Stop Dev instances on a schedule** (save ~65% by running only 12h/day):
```bash
# EventBridge rule to stop instances at 7 PM
aws events put-rule --name StopDevInstances --schedule-expression "cron(0 19 ? * MON-FRI *)"
```

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between Reserved Instances and Savings Plans?:::Reserved Instances commit to a specific instance type, AZ, and OS for 1 or 3 years, giving up to 72% savings. Savings Plans commit to a dollar-per-hour spend rate for 1 or 3 years and automatically apply to any matching usage (any EC2 family, any region for Compute Savings Plans, or Fargate and Lambda). Savings Plans are more flexible and simpler to manage because you do not need to specify exact instance types upfront. AWS now recommends Savings Plans over RIs for most use cases.

When should you use Spot Instances and what is the risk?:::Spot Instances are ideal for fault-tolerant, stateless, or checkpointing workloads: batch data processing, EMR jobs, CI/CD build agents, ML training with checkpointing, and rendering. The risk is that AWS can reclaim Spot capacity with a 2-minute warning when demand for that instance type increases. Applications must handle interruptions gracefully — save state, drain connections, or use Auto Scaling with multiple instance types and On-Demand base capacity to maintain availability.

How does AWS Compute Optimizer recommend rightsizing?:::Compute Optimizer analyzes 14 days of CloudWatch utilization metrics (CPU utilization, network in/out, disk read/write for EC2; memory if CloudWatch agent is installed). It compares actual usage against the instance type's capacity and recommends: downsize if CPU < 40% consistently, upsize if CPU > 90%, or switch to a different family (e.g., from m5 to c5 for CPU-intensive workloads or r5 for memory-intensive). It provides estimated monthly savings for each recommendation.

Explain S3 storage tiers and when to use each.:::S3 Standard ($0.023/GB) is for frequently accessed data. Standard-IA ($0.0125/GB) is for data accessed less than once a month — lower storage cost but a retrieval fee. Glacier Instant Retrieval ($0.004/GB) is for archive data with millisecond access needs. Glacier Deep Archive ($0.001/GB) is the lowest cost for data accessed less than once a year with 12-hour retrieval tolerance. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between tiers based on access patterns — ideal when access is unpredictable.

What are cost allocation tags and why are they important?:::Cost allocation tags are key-value pairs you attach to AWS resources (EC2, RDS, S3, etc.). After activation in the Billing console, they appear in the Cost and Usage Report, allowing you to break down costs by team, project, environment, or cost center. Without tags, you see a single AWS bill with no insight into which product or team drove the cost. Tags enable chargebacks (billing teams for their usage), budgets per project, and identification of cost anomalies by service.

What is Cost Anomaly Detection and how does it differ from billing alarms?:::A billing alarm triggers when the month-to-date estimated charge exceeds a fixed dollar threshold. Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning to establish a baseline spending pattern per service (or by linked account, cost category, or tag) and alerts when spending deviates unexpectedly — even if the absolute amount is below your billing alarm threshold. For example, if EC2 normally costs $500/day and suddenly spikes to $2,000/day, Anomaly Detection catches it even if the monthly total is still within your alarm threshold. It is more sensitive for catching sudden unexpected charges.

quizBank.question: Which AWS feature provides automatic failover for RDS within the same region when the primary DB instance fails?
quizBank.explanation: RDS Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ. On failure, AWS automatically updates the DNS endpoint to point to the standby within 1-2 minutes. Read Replicas are asynchronous and used for read scaling, not automatic failover.

quizBank.question: An S3 presigned URL is generated server-side. Which of the following is true about its security?
options: A) It requires the client to have AWS credentials
B) It embeds the signer's credentials and expires after a set time
C) It bypasses bucket policies entirely
D) It can only be used for GET requests
quizBank.explanation: A presigned URL uses AWS Signature V4 to embed the signer's identity and an expiration timestamp into the URL. The client needs no AWS credentials — the signature proves authorization. Bucket policies still apply; deny rules on the bucket can still block access. Presigned URLs can be used for PUT (upload) as well as GET.

quizBank.question: What is a Lambda cold start, and which configuration reduces it most effectively for latency-sensitive workloads?
quizBank.explanation: A cold start occurs when Lambda must initialize a new execution environment — downloading code, starting the runtime, and running initialization code outside the handler. This adds latency (100ms to 1s+ for JVM). For latency-sensitive workloads, Provisioned Concurrency keeps N environments warm and initialized, eliminating cold starts entirely. Other mitigations: keep package size small, use Lambda SnapStart for Java, avoid heavy initialization in handler.

quizBank.question: You have a DynamoDB table with userId as partition key. You need to query orders by status across all users. What do you add?
options: A) Local Secondary Index on status
B) Global Secondary Index with status as partition key
C) Scan the table with a filter expression
D) Add status as a sort key to the base table
quizBank.explanation: A GSI allows a completely different partition key (status) and sort key, enabling efficient queries across all users by status. LSI requires the same partition key as the base table, so it cannot query across all users. Scans read every item and are expensive. Changing the base table's sort key would only let you query per-user by status.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between a Security Group and a Network ACL (NACL) in a VPC?
quizBank.explanation: Security Groups are stateful (return traffic is automatically allowed), operate at the instance/ENI level, and only support allow rules. NACLs are stateless (you must explicitly allow inbound AND outbound for each flow), operate at the subnet level, and support both allow and deny rules. Use Security Groups for most cases; NACLs add a subnet-level explicit deny layer (e.g., blocking a specific IP range).

quizBank.question: Which SQS queue type guarantees exactly-once processing and strict message ordering?
quizBank.explanation: SQS FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing (deduplication via message deduplication ID) and strict first-in-first-out ordering within a message group. Standard queues offer at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering with virtually unlimited throughput. FIFO queues support up to 3000 messages/second with batching.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a NAT Gateway and where must it be placed?
quizBank.explanation: A NAT Gateway allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound internet connections (e.g., to download packages or call external APIs) while preventing the internet from initiating inbound connections to those instances. It must be placed in a public subnet (one with a route to an Internet Gateway). Private subnet route tables then route 0.0.0.0/0 to the NAT Gateway. For HA, deploy one NAT Gateway per AZ.

quizBank.question: In AWS IAM, what is an IAM Role and how does it differ from an IAM User?
quizBank.explanation: An IAM User has permanent long-term credentials (username/password, access keys) and represents a specific person or service account. An IAM Role has no permanent credentials — instead it provides temporary security credentials via STS when assumed. Roles are assumed by AWS services (EC2 instance profile, Lambda execution role), other IAM users (cross-account access), or federated users. Best practice: assign roles to services, never embed access keys in code.

quizBank.question: An application processes images uploaded to S3. Which architecture is most cost-effective and scalable?
options: A) EC2 instance polling S3 with a cron job every minute
B) S3 Event Notification → SNS → SQS → Lambda
C) EC2 Auto Scaling Group reading directly from S3
D) CloudWatch Events polling S3 bucket for changes
quizBank.explanation: S3 Event Notification → SNS → SQS → Lambda is the canonical event-driven pattern. S3 publishes an event on upload, SNS fans it out (enabling multiple consumers), SQS provides buffering and retry with DLQ, and Lambda processes without any idle server cost. Option A wastes money on an always-on EC2 polling loop. Lambda scales automatically to concurrency without managing servers.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a DynamoDB GSI's projection and how does it affect cost?
quizBank.explanation: A GSI projection defines which attributes are copied from the base table into the index. Options: KEYS_ONLY (just PK+SK), INCLUDE (specific attributes), ALL (every attribute). Projecting only what your query needs reduces index storage cost and read capacity units consumed. Projecting ALL is convenient but doubles storage and read cost for large items. Design rule: project only the attributes your access pattern actually reads.

quizBank.question: What is the primary advantage of AWS Cloud Services?
options: D) All of the above, depending on context
quizBank.explanation: AWS Cloud Services offers different advantages depending on the use case. In interviews, always discuss trade-offs rather than absolving advantages.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use AWS Cloud Services?
options: B) When the team is small
C) When the overhead exceeds the benefit
quizBank.explanation: Every technology has trade-offs. AWS Cloud Services adds complexity that may not be justified for simple use cases. Always evaluate if the overhead is worth the benefit.

quizBank.question: What is the time complexity of the core operation in AWS Cloud Services?
options: D) It depends on the implementation
quizBank.explanation: The time complexity depends on the specific implementation and operation. In interviews, always clarify which operation before stating complexity.

quizBank.question: How does AWS Cloud Services handle failures?
options: D) Strategy depends on the system requirements
quizBank.explanation: Failure handling in AWS Cloud Services depends on requirements: retry for transient failures, circuit breaker for cascading failures, graceful degradation for user-facing systems.

quizBank.question: Which company popularized the use of AWS Cloud Services?
quizBank.explanation: AWS Cloud Services evolved through contributions from multiple companies. In interviews, mentioning real-world examples from different companies shows breadth of knowledge.
